coredata.dc:title: Lysosome-targetable carbon dots for highly efficient photothermal/photodynamic synergistic cancer therapy and photoacoustic/two-photon excited fluorescence imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Shaojing
Wu, Shuilin
Jia, Qingyan
Huang, Li
Lan, Minhuan
Wang, Pengfei
Zhang, Wenjun
coredata.dc:description: Phototherapy, including photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT) holds great potential for efficient cancer therapy by inducing reactive oxygen species (ROS) or heat into tumor. Nevertheless, PDT or PTT suffers from some limitations, e.g., PTT requires long-time and high-power laser irradiation to generate enough heat, while the hypoxia microenvironment of tumor and the limit diffuse distance of ROS hamper the efficacy of oxygen-dependent PDT. Here we reported the carbon dots (CDs) which could simultaneously generate singlet oxygen (1O2), hydroxyl radical (OH), and heat under a 635 nm laser irradiation, with a 1O2 generation quantum yield of 5.7% and photothermal conversion efficiency of 73.5% (the highest thus far for CDs). Significantly, the CDs can selectively accumulate in lysosome, which is an ideal organelle for phototherapy because of its key role in sustaining cellular activity and stability. Moreover, the CDs present one-photon excited (OPE) and two-photon excited (TPE) fluorescence, and excellent photoacoustic (PA) imaging capability. Combining the good biocompatibility, the as-prepared CDs was served as multi-functional phototheranostic agent for synergistic PA/fluorescence imaging, and PDT/PTT.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
PDT
PTT
Two-photon excited
Photoacoustic imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vivo thermal imaging and phototherapy
ROS generation and photothermal conversion capability
Lysosome-targeting fluorescence imaging in living cells
In vivo PA/photothermal imaging and phototherapy

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, the inset shows the HRTEM image of one CD, (b) AFM image, (c) XRD pattern, and (d) Raman spectrum of the CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, deconvolution of high-resolution (b) C1s and (c) N1s XPS spectra. (d) FTIR spectrum.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs aqueous solution. The insets show the photograph (left) and fluorescent image (right) of CDs aqueous solution under an UV light (365 nm) irradiation. (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelengths excitation. (c) Fluorescence spectra of CDs obtained under 800 nm laser excitation with different laser powers (from 145 to 397 mW). (d) Quadratic relationship between the integrated fluorescence spectrum and the laser power.

ESR spectra of (a) CDs + TEMP and (b) CDs + BMPO mixture solutions before and after 635 nm laser irradiation (2 W/cm2). (c) Time-dependent temperature variation of CDs solutions with different concentration (from 0 to 200 μg/mL) under 635 nm laser irradiation (2 W/cm2). (d) Concentration-dependent temperature change (ΔT) of the CDs solution after different power laser irradiation (1 W/cm2 and 2 W/cm2) for 10 min.

(a) Bright field, (b) OPE (λex = 457 nm) and (c) TPE (λex = 800 nm) fluorescence images of CDs incubated HeLa cells. Scale bar: 75 nm. (d) Bright field image, and fluorescence images collected in (e) FITC channel (λex = 457 nm, collection: 500–600 nm) and (f) Cy5 channel (λex = 635 nm, collection: 650–750 nm) of HeLa cells co-stained with 50 μg/mL CDs and 100 nM LysoTracker Deep Red. (g) The merged images of (e and f). (h) Fluorescence intensity profile within the yellow line in (g). (i) The fluorescence intensity correlation plot of CDs (FITC channel) and LysoTracker Deep Red (Cy5 channel). The Pearson’s correlation coefficient was calculated to be 0.89.

Fluorescent images of Calcein-AM/PI co-stained CDs incubated HeLa cells (a) before and (b) after 635 nm laser treatment for 10 min. (c) The CDs’ concentration-dependent cell viability in the dark condition, and exposed to a 635 nm laser (with different laser powers) irradiation for 10 min.

PA images of the mouse (a) before and (b) after injection of 50 μL CDs (1 mg/mL) for 4 h. (c) Thermal images of the CDs injected mouse before and after 800 nm laser (500 mW) for 10 min. (d) Time-dependent tumor volumes after different treatments.
Schematic illustration of CDs as a multifunctional phototheranostic agent for dual fluorescence and PA imaging, and synergistic PDT and PTT under laser irradiation.

head.ce:title: Lysosome-targetable carbon dots for highly efficient photothermal/photodynamic synergistic cancer therapy and photoacoustic/two-photon excited fluorescence imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Conversion and Optoelectronic Materials and CityU-CAS Joint Laboratory of Functional Materials and Devices, Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry (TIPC), Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Conversion and Optoelectronic Materials and CityU-CAS Joint Laboratory of Functional Materials and Devices
Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry (TIPC)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Conversion and Optoelectronic Materials and CityU-CAS Joint Laboratory of Functional Materials and Devices, Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry (TIPC), Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lysosome targetable carbon dots which can simultaneously generate 1O2, OH and heat under 635 nm laser irradiation were prepared, their applications in photothermal/photodynamic synergistic cancer therapy and photoacoustic/two-photon excited fluorescence imaging were demonstrated.

Phototherapy, including photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT) holds great potential for efficient cancer therapy by inducing reactive oxygen species (ROS) or heat into tumor. Nevertheless, PDT or PTT suffers from some limitations, e.g., PTT requires long-time and high-power laser irradiation to generate enough heat, while the hypoxia microenvironment of tumor and the limit diffuse distance of ROS hamper the efficacy of oxygen-dependent PDT. Here we reported the carbon dots (CDs) which could simultaneously generate singlet oxygen (1O2), hydroxyl radical (OH), and heat under a 635 nm laser irradiation, with a 1O2 generation quantum yield of 5.7% and photothermal conversion efficiency of 73.5% (the highest thus far for CDs). Significantly, the CDs can selectively accumulate in lysosome, which is an ideal organelle for phototherapy because of its key role in sustaining cellular activity and stability. Moreover, the CDs present one-photon excited (OPE) and two-photon excited (TPE) fluorescence, and excellent photoacoustic (PA) imaging capability. Combining the good biocompatibility, the as-prepared CDs was served as multi-functional phototheranostic agent for synergistic PA/fluorescence imaging, and PDT/PTT.

ce:section.ce:para: Phototherapy has been demonstrated as a promising technique for cancer therapy, e.g., photothermal therapy (PTT) exploits the heat produced from the photothermal agent (PTA) exposed to light to cook cells [1], and photodynamic therapy (PDT) utilizes photosensitizer (PS) generated reactive oxygen species (ROS) to destroy cancer cells under light irradiation [2]. Compared with the traditional cancer therapy approaches like surgery, chemotherapy, and radiation therapy, phototherapy has its superiorities including high selectivity and high efficiency of tumor destruction, no drug resistance, noninvasive feature, and easy to combine with other therapeutic approaches [3,4]. However, PTT requires high-power and long-time laser irradiation to produce adequate heat to kill cells, and the up-regulated expression of heat shock proteins greatly decreases the treatment effect [5]. On the other hand, the therapeutic outcome of PDT is generally discounted because of the hypoxia tumor microenvironment [6,7]. Moreover, PDT will consume oxygen and thus further aggravate tumor hypoxia [8,9].

Integrating multiple therapy assays into one system, e.g., especially synergistic PDT and PTT could avoid the drawback of the mono-mode therapy. A variety of PTAs, including gold- [10], carbon- [11,12], semiconductor-based nanomaterials [13,14], and organic dyes [15,16] have been used to combine with commercial PSs to construct PDT and PTT theranostic agents. Although remarkable advances have been achieved, most of these agents need two lasers with different wavelength to activate PTT and PDT separately [17,18]. Moreover, the shortcomings of PTA, including high cytotoxicity of metal-containing PTAs, poor photostability and tumor selectivity, and low photothermal conversion efficiency of organic dyes limit their biomedical applications; for PDT, due to the high activity of ROSs in living cells, the cytotoxic response of PDT is restricted at the local areas surrounding PSs [19,20].

As a fascinating fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have great potential in biomedical application due to the advantages of easy preparation and surface modification, excellent water solubility, photostability and biocompatibility [21–23]. Moreover, CDs also could generate ROSs or heat under light irradiation [24–27]. In our previous work, N,S-co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S-GQDs) prepared from polythiophene derivate (PT1) exhibited a 1O2 generation quantum yield of 130% under white light irradiation, which is the highest reported thus far. However, the photothermal effect of the N,S-GQDs was negligible [28]. Using diphenyl diselenide and PT1 as the carbon sources, near infrared (NIR) fluorescent S,Se-doped CDs were synthesized via the same hydrothermal method. Interestingly, the obtained CDs could not produce 1O2 under laser or white light excitation, but showed a high photothermal conversion efficiency of 58.2%. Moreover, the large two-photon absorption cross-section enable the capability of two-photon-excitation (TPE) fluorescence imaging and photothermal therapy [29]. The factors such as the precursors for synthesizing CDs, microstructure, and the incorporated dopants, were revealed to have predominant impacts on the photothermal or photodynamic activities [30,31]. Moreover, the CDs reported thus far for PDT or PTT are unable to target important subcellular organelles, and their ROS generation and photothermal conversion efficacy need to be further improved to satisfy the clinical requirement. Especially, the oxygen-independent ROS generation capability is more desirable in PDT due to the hypoxia tumor microenvironment.

In this study, yellow fluorescent CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of trinitropyrene in 100 mM NaOH aqueous solution. The fluorescence peaked at ~570 nm of the CDs aqueous solution was obtained under one-photon excited (OPE) or two-photon excited (TPE) mode. Fluorescence imaging in living cells revealed that the CDs could be excited by both 457 nm and 800 nm lasers, and could be selectively accumulated in lysosomes. The delivery of phototherapy agent into lysosome is helpful for improving the therapeutic effect because lysosomes are the recycling centers of the cell [32]. Particularly, the CDs could convert the light energy into heat, and simultaneously generate 1O2 and OH under 635 nm laser irradiation (Scheme 1 ). The photothermal conversion efficiency and 1O2 generation quantum yield of the CDs were measured to be ~73.5% (the highest reported till now for the CDs-based PTAs [33–37]) and 5.7%, respectively. Moreover, due to the broad absorbance and strong photothermal conversion ability, the CDs showed excellent photoacoustic (PA) and thermal imaging performance. These combined outstanding properties enable the CDs to be applied as excellent multi-modes imaging and promising PDT and PTT agent.

The mixture solution of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (10 mg) and NaOH (100 mM, 15 mL) was ultrasonicated for 60 min, and heated at 200 °C for 12 h in an autoclave. The CDs were further purified through sequential filtration, centrifugation, and dialysis procedures.

TEM images were collected using the JEOL JEM-2100F electron microscope. AFM images were obtained from Nano-ScopeIIIa MultiMode AFM. XPS measurements were conducted by using a VG ESCALAB 220i-XL surface analysis system. Raman spectra were measured using an Invia-Reflex Raman system using a 633 nm laser. XRD was performed using an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) with Cu-Ka radiation. FTIR were obtained by an IR spectrophotometer (IFS 66 V/S, Bruker, Germany).

The absorption and OPE fluorescence spectra were measured by Shimadzu 2600 spectrophotometer and HITACHI F4600 spectrophotometer, respectively. TPE fluorescence spectra were collected on a fiber spectrometer (Ocean Optics USB2000 CCD) under a Ti: sapphire femtosecond laser excitation (Spitfire, Spectra-Physics, 100 fs, 80 MHz).

The 1O2 luminescence spectra of CDs and methylene blue (MB) in D2O were measured in the HITACHI F900 fluorescence spectrophotometer under a 635 nm laser excitation. The 1O2 quantum yield of the CDs (
ϕ
CDs
) was calculated by Eq. (e1):
(e1)
ϕ
CDs
=
ϕ
MB
F
CDs
F
MB
A
MB
A
CDs
where F is the integral area of the 1O2 luminescence spectrum, A is the absorbance at 635 nm,
ϕ
MB
is the 1O2 generation quantum yield of MB, and
ϕ
MB
= 0.52
in D2O.

ESR spectra were obtained on the ESP300E spectrometer (Bruker). 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) and 5-tert-butoxycarbonyl-5-methyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (BMPO) were used as the 1O2 and OH trapper, respectively. 0.1 mL CDs aqueous solution (20 μg/mL) were added with 3 μL TEMP or BMPO. The mixture was added into a capillary tube. The ESR spectra of the sample before and after laser irradiation were obtained. As the control experiment, 0.1 mL DI water was replaced with the CDs aqueous solution.

The photothermal conversion capability of CDs was analyzed by irradiation a quartz cuvette containing aqueous solution of CDs with different concentrations using 635 nm or 800 nm laser. The temperatures of the aqueous solutions were measured and recorded by a digital thermometer.

HeLa cells were incubated with 50 μg/mL CDs for 4 h at 37 °C, washed with PBS for three times, and then imaged by a laser scanning confocal microscopy under 457 nm or 800 nm laser excitation. For the colocalization of CDs and the lysosome tracker, HeLa cells were incubated with 50 μg/mL CDs for 4 h and then LysoTracker Deep Red (100 nM) for 30 min.

Calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI) co-staining test was performed to monitor the in vitro phototherapy efficiency of CDs. HeLa cells were incubated with CDs (100 μg/mL) for 4 h and then exposed to 635 nm laser irradiation for 10 min. Afterwards, a mixture solution containing 2 μM calcein-AM and 5 μM PI was added, and the cells were incubated for another 30 min, washed with PBS, and then fluorescence imaged to distinct the proportion of live/dead cells. MTT assay was used to investigate the cytotoxicity of CDs under different concentrations and different laser powers irradiation.

Animal experiments were performed following the guideline of animal use and care of Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. The mice were subcutaneously injected with 4T1 cells. After one week, 50 μL CDs (1 mg/mL) was intravenously injected into the mice. The PA imaging were performed after 8 h post injection using a MOST Invision128 PA tomography system (iThera Medical, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo thermal imaging and phototherapy
ce:section.ce:para: 20 4T1 tumor bearing mice were randomly separated into four groups (n = 5), including one phototherapy group and three control groups. 80 μL of CDs (1 mg/mL) solution or saline was intratumorally injected the tumors. After 1 h, the tumors were exposed to 800 nm laser (500 mW) irradiation for 10 min. In the same time, full body thermal images were collected by a Fluke Ti400 IR Camera. Tumor volumes were calculated based on the formula: V = a × b2/2 (a: length of tumor; b: width of tumor).

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image in Fig. 1 a shows that the CDs are well-dispersed and uniform in size. Clear crystal structure with the lattice spacing of ~0.20 nm which is corresponding to the (1 0 2) planes of graphitic carbon was observed in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset in Fig. 1a)[38]. According to the statistical analysis of 50 CDs from TEM image, the average diameter of the CDs is ~2.5 ± 0.5 nm (Fig. S1a). The atomic force microscopy (AFM) image shown in Fig. 1b reveals that the CDs have a height of ~2.5 nm (Fig. S1b), further suggesting the spherical shape of CDs. The microstructure of CDs is confirmed by powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD), and the characteristic (0 0 2) graphitic carbon peak at 2θ = 27.2° is observed, as shown in Fig. 1c. Moreover, the disordered D band (peak at 1347 cm−1) and crystalline G band (peak at 1575 cm−1) were observed in the Raman spectrum (Fig. 1d), and a relative intensity ratio (ID/IG) of 1.05 was calculated, suggesting the CDs have a graphite-like structure [39].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze the chemical composition of the CDs. The survey XPS spectrum in Fig. 2 a presented six peaks, assigned to C1s, N1s, Na KLL, O1s, O KLL and Na1s, respectively. The Na signals were observed due to the high concentration of NaOH aqueous solution was used in the synthesis procedure, and excluding Na, the atomic ratio of C/N/O was calculated to be 76.4/3.4/20.2. The HR C1s XPS spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2b) was deconvoluted into four components, i.e., CC, CN, CO, and CO with the binding energies at 284.6, 285.2, 286.4, and 288.8 eV, respectively. Three components with binding energies at 399.0 eV for pyridinic N, 400.0 eV for pyrrolic N, and 400.9 eV for NH were resolved in the HR N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c). Moreover, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy in Fig. 2d provided more evidence for the presence of the above mentioned functional groups, e.g., the peaks at ~1640, ~1580, ~1378, and ~1045 cm−1 raised from CO, CC, CN, and CO bonds, respectively, were observed. The strong peak at ~3400 cm−1 maybe originated from the vibration of OH. In addition, negative Zeta potential of −31.9 mV was detected, demonstrating the presence of carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs in Fig. 3 a showed a broad band from the visible region to over 800 nm. The CDs aqueous solution emitted yellow fluorescence under the 365 nm light irradiation (inset in Fig. 3a), with a fluorescence quantum yield of 4.9%. A fluorescence emission peak centered at ~570 nm was obtained under 500 nm excitation, and the fluorescence spectra show excitation wavelength-independent characteristic (Fig. 3b). Moreover, the fluorescence spectra of the CDs in water/organic solvent mixtures (v/v = 20/1), including EtOH, DMSO, CH3CN and THF were also collected. As demonstrated in Fig. S2, no obvious emission wavelength shifting was observed as increasing the excitation wavelength. As compared with in pure water, the emission wavelengths of CDs in these mixtures shows a slight red-shift. The time-resolved fluorescence spectrum of the CDs in water showed three exponential decays, with the longest lifetime of 6.83 ns (Fig. S3a), suggesting the singlet excited state emission of the CDs. The CDs also displayed excellent photostability as compared with organic dye. As shown in Fig. S3b and c, no obvious changes were observed in the UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs aqueous solution after 60 min light irradiation, while the absorbance of fluorescein disodium was decreased from 0.70 to ~0.15. The TPE fluorescence spectrum of the CDs was measured under an 800 nm femtosecond pulsed laser excitation. As shown in Fig. 3c, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was increased upon increasing the laser power, and a good quadratic relationship with a slop of 1.09 between the integrated fluorescence spectrum area and the laser power (Fig. 2d) [40,41]. Moreover, the emission wavelength of the CDs under 800 nm excitation is the same as that of under 400 nm excitation (Fig. S3d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS generation and photothermal conversion capability
ce:section.ce:para: Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was carried out to detect the ROS generation from CDs, and 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) and 5-tert-butoxycarbonyl-5-methyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (BMPO) were used to trap 1O2 and OH, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3a and b, after exposed to 635 nm laser irradiation for 10 min, the ESR signals of TEMP adduct with 1O2 (nitroxide radical) and BMPO adducts with OH were detected in the CDs + TEMP and CDs + BMPO mixture solutions, respectively. However, the ESR spectra of the CDs-free solutions show little change after laser irradiation (Fig. S4). These results indicated that the CDs could simultaneously produce 1O2 and OH under 635 nm laser irradiation. Moreover, the characteristic photoluminescence of 1O2 peaked at ~1275 nm was also observed under 635 nm laser excitation (Fig. S4). Using methylene blue (MB) as the standard reference, the 1O2 generation quantum yield of the CDs was measured to be ~5.7% (Fig. S5).

The photothermal conversion property of CDs was studied by analyzing the increase in temperature of CDs solutions upon laser irradiation. As revealed in Fig. 3c, after receive 10 min laser irradiation, the CDs solutions with a concentration of 12.5, 25, 50, 100 and 200 μg/mL demonstrated significant temperature increase to 45.6, 52.3, 57.3, 65.3, and 74.7 °C, respectively. However, the temperature of CDs-free solution only increased to 38.2 °C under the same laser irradiation. The temperature increases also depended on the power of laser. As shown in Fig. 4 d, the higher power was used, the more significant temperature increased. The photothermal conversion efficiency was calculated to be ~73.5% (Fig. S6), which is the highest among the CDs reported thus far [33–37]. More importantly, the CDs show excellent photostability, after 10 cycles of laser ON/OFF procedures, a temperature increase of ~35 °C was still be achieved (Fig. S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lysosome-targeting fluorescence imaging in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular fluorescence imaging performance of CDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 , CDs incubated HeLa cells exhibited green fluorescence under either 457 nm or 800 nm laser excitation, and a better spatial resolution was observed under TPE mode. The capability of a PS to selectively target the important subcellular organelles, such as endosomes, lysosomes, mitochondria, and the nucleus is a significant merit to enhance its efficiency in phototherapy [42–48]. You et al. utilized a double endoplasmic reticulum (ER)-targeting strategy to realize PDT and PTT immunotherapy. Compared with the non-targeting nanosystem, the ER-targeting nanosystem was shown to have robust ER stress and calreticulin exposure on the cell surface under NIR light irradiation, and thus delivered an enhanced anti-tumor efficacy [49]. However, the samples needed multi-step chemical modification, and the in vivo structural stability of these nanocomposites should also be concerned.

Although previous reports demonstrated that the morpholine modified CDs could target lysosome, only a few CDs reported thus far to be able to specifically image the lysosomes [50–52]. It was reported that the hydrophilic CDs containing amine groups have the intrinsic ability to target lysosomes in living cells [53]. To study the subcellular localization ability of the as-prepared CDs, HeLa cells were co-incubated with CDs and a commercial lysosome targeting dye (LysoTracker Deep Red). Fig. 5d depicts the bright field image of two HeLa cells after incubation with CDs and LysoTracker Deep Red. The fluorescent field images in Fig. 5e and f exhibit green and red fluorescence inside the cells, which are originated from the CDs and LysoTracker Deep Red, respectively. Particularly, the green and red fluorescence signals overlap very well with each other (Fig. 5g and h). The Pearson’s correlation coefficient was calculated to be ~0.89 (Fig. 5i), indicating these CDs could indeed target the lysosomes in living cells.

To visualize the phototherapeutic effect of CDs, the CDs incubated HeLa cells were exposed to a 635 nm laser irradiation, and sequentially co-stained with Calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI) fluorescent dyes which could selectively label the living and dead cells, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 a, bright green fluorescence signal was detected in the CDs incubated HeLa cells before laser treatment. However, after irradiation for 10 min, the cells displayed red fluorescence (Fig. 6b), illustrating the effectiveness of the heat and ROSs generated by the CDs exposed to light irradiation. In contrast, no obvious red fluorescence was observed in the cells in the absence of CDs before and after laser irradiation (Fig. S8). Subsequently, methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) assay was carried to quantitatively evaluate the in vitro PTT and PDT efficacies of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 6c, the cell viability showed strong dependent on the irradiation time, laser power, and CDs concentration. Obvious phototherapy efficacy was observed in the CDs + laser irradiation groups, i.e., even receiving 25 μg/mL CDs dose and 10 min irradiation with the power of 2.0 W/cm2, the cell viability decreased to ~50%. We noted that the cell viability was sharp decreased when the CDs concentration increased from 50 to 100 μg/mL under 1.0 W/cm2 laser irradiation. This observation agreed well with the previous results that only the temperature was higher than 46 °C could induce the irreversible cellular damage.47 As shown in Fig. 4d, the temperatures of the CDs’ solutions with the concentration of 50 and 100 μg/mL were increased to 43 and 50 °C, respectively. In dark condition, the cell viabilities were large than 80% when the concentration of CDs less than 100 μg/mL. The above results demonstrated that the CDs have low cytotoxicity and high phototoxicity.

Furthermore, the separately PDT and PTT effects of CDs on the therapeutic was further studied. The HeLa cell incubated with CDs and exposed to 635 nm laser irradiation with a low power of 0.1 W/cm2 for 10 min. In this case, the photothermal effect of the CDs could be negligible, and only ~20% cells were destroyed by the PDT mechanism (Fig. S9). Moreover, it is reported that the vitamin C can significantly protect the cells from PDT treatment.31 In order to eliminate the PDT effect of CDs, the HeLa cells were co-incubated with CDs and 200 μM vitamin C, and then irradiated by the 635 nm laser with a high power of 2 W/cm2. MTT results demonstrated that ~50% cells were killed in the presence of 100 μg/mL CDs (Figure S9). It should be noted that in the absence of vitamin C, the cell viability of HeLa cells incubated with 100 μg/mL CDs and received 2 W/cm2 laser irradiation for 10 min was decreased to ~10% (Fig. 6c). These results strongly indicated the synergistic effect of PDT and PTT could significantly improve the therapeutics.

Moreover, the CDs also showed excellent photothermal conversion capability even under 800 nm laser irradiation. As shown in Fig. S10a, after 800 nm laser irradiation for 10 min, the temperature of the CDs aqueous solutions with concentration of 100 and 200 μg/mL was increased to 40.0 and 47.8 °C, respectively. However, the temperature of the CDs-free solution only increased to ~34.5 °C. The MTT result shown in Fig. S10b revealed that the cell viability of the cells incubated with 200 μg/mL CDs was decreased to ~20% after laser irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo PA/photothermal imaging and phototherapy
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the broad absorption spectrum and high photothermal conversion efficiency, the in vivo PA imaging performance of CDs on the 4T1-tumor bearing nude mice was studied. As shown in Fig. 7 a and b, the PA signal in the tumor region was significantly enhanced after intravenous injection of CDs for 4 h. The in vivo phototherapy performance of CDs was further investigated by using 800 nm laser. As shown in Fig. 7c, after injection of CDs and exposed to laser irradiation for 10 min, the temperature of the tumor was increased to ~70 °C, while the temperature of the tumor injected with saline only increased by 5 °C after the same laser treatment. Based on that, 20 tumor bearing mice were randomly separated into four groups to investigate the in vivo phototherapy performance of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 7d, the tumor growth of the CDs + Laser group was efficiently inhibited, while the tumors in the three control groups (i.e., only laser treatment, only injection of CDs, and only injection of saline) showed rapid growth, indicating that solely laser irradiation or CDs injection could not destroy the tumor. These results agreed well with that observed in vitro, and clarified that the CDs could serve as an efficient NIR laser triggered phototherapeutic agent.

In conclusion, fluorescent CDs which can simultaneously produce 1O2, OH and heat under 635 nm laser irradiation were synthesized, and the highest photothermal conversion efficiency of 73.5% was obtained. The OPE and TPE fluorescence HeLa cells imaging revealed that the CDs could selectively accumulate in lysosome. These outstanding merits enable the CDs to be served as a lysosome targetable phototheranostic agent for the simultaneous PA and fluorescence imaging, and PDT/PTT of cancers.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the External Cooperation Program of Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. GJHZ1723), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61720106014 and 61805287), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province, China (No. 2018JJ3632 and 2019JJ50824), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 202045002), Basic Research Foundation of Shenzhen Science and Technology Innovation (No. JCYJ20180307151245919), and the General Research Fund of Hong Kong (GRF. No. CityU 11306717, and CityU 11338516).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Particle size distribution histograms, AFM image, time-resolved fluorescence decay spectrum and ESR spectra of CDs. 1O2 generation quantum yield and photothermal conversion efficiency calculation, photothermal stability of CDs. photothermal conversion and MTT assay of A549 cells under 800 nm laser irradiation.
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.124212.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photothermal therapy and photoacoustic imaging via nanotheranostics in fighting cancer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, P. Bhattarai, Z. Dai, X. Chen, Photothermal therapy and photoacoustic imaging via nanotheranostics in fighting cancer. Chem. Soc. Rev. 48 (2019) 2053-2108.
